Apply **momentum theorem** to derive $T = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (V_e^2 - V_0^2)$: 2 marks - Specialize to **hover** ($V_0 = 0$), obtain $T = 2 \rho A V_i^2$ and $V_i = \sqrt{T / (2\rho A)}$: 1 mark - Apply **energy conservation** to derive $P_{\text{hover}} = T \cdot V_i = T^{3/2} / \sqrt{2\rho A}$: 1 mark **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Given Data:** - $M = 3.5 \text{ kg}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $N_{\text{motors}} = 6$ - $D = 12 \text{ in} = 12 \times 0.0254 = 0.3048 \text{ m}$ - $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1: Total Weight** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= M \cdot g = 3.5 \times 9.81 = 34.335 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Thrust per Motor** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{motor}} &= \frac{W}{6} = \frac{34.335}{6} = 5.7225 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Disk Area** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.3048)^2}{4} = 0.07297 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Induced Velocity** $$ \begin{aligned} V_i &= \sqrt{\frac{T_{\text{motor}}}{2 \rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.7225}{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.07297}} \\ V_i &= \sqrt{\frac{5.7225}{0.17877}} = \sqrt{32.01} = 5.658 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Total Ideal Hover Power** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{motor}} &= T_{\text{motor}} \cdot V_i = 5.7225 \times 5.658 = 32.38 \text{ W} \\ P_{\text{total}} &= 6 \times 32.38 = 194.3 \text{ W} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{motor}} &\approx 5.72 \text{ N} \\ V_i &\approx 5.66 \text{ m/s} \\ P_{\text{total}} &\approx 194.3 \text{ W} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` > **(a)** Describe the **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** used in multirotor UAV propulsion. Explain the role of the **KV rating**, **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)**, and **LiPo battery C-rating** in determining the propulsion performance. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A drone uses a 4S LiPo battery (nominal 14.8 V, 5000 mAh, 25C) and a BLDC motor with a KV rating of 920. The propeller diameter is 9 inches. Estimate the **no-load RPM**, **maximum continuous current draw**, and the **theoretical flight time** assuming ideal hover power of 150 W. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for Part (a):** - Briefly introduce BLDC motor construction (stator + rotor + Hall sensors/ESC commutation): 2 marks - Explain KV rating: $N_0 = KV \times V_{\text{batt}}$; high KV = fast/low torque, low KV = slow/high torque: 2 marks - ESC function: converts DC to 3-phase AC, modulates frequency to control RPM: 1.5 marks - LiPo C-rating: defines max safe discharge current $I_{\max} = C_{\text{rate}} \times \text{Capacity}$: 1.5 marks **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Given:** - 4S → $V_{\text{nom}} = 14.8 \text{ V}$ - $C = 5000 \text{ mAh} = 5.0 \text{ Ah}$, $C_{\text{rate}} = 25$ - $KV = 920 \text{ RPM/V}$ **Step 1: No-load RPM** $$ \begin{aligned} N_0 &= KV \cdot V_{\text{nom}} = 920 \times 14.8 = 13616 \text{ RPM} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Maximum Continuous Current** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\max} &= C_{\text{rate}} \times C = 25 \times 5.0 = 125 \text{ A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Flight Time** Battery energy: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{bat}} &= V_{\text{nom}} \times C = 14.8 \times 5.0 = 74 \text{ Wh} \end{aligned} $$ Flight time at constant 150 W power draw: $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{flight}} &= \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{74}{150} = 0.4933 \text{ h} \approx 29.6 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** > **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget that a "4S" LiPo is a *series* configuration of 4 cells, giving $4 \times 3.7 = 14.8 \text{ V}$ nominal, **not** the fully charged $4 \times 4.2 = 16.8 \text{ V}$. If the question specifies "nominal," use 3.7 V/cell. If it says "fully charged," use 4.2 V/cell. > > **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **KV rating** with **kV (kilovolts)**. KV in motor terminology is **RPM per Volt**, not to be confused with the unit of electric potential difference. > > **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to multiply the motor count when computing total system power. Always remember: *4 motors × 150 W = 600 W* if the question says "each motor requires 150 W." > > **Common Mistake #4:** In the propulsion chain diagram, students often forget to draw the **feedback loop** from the battery's state-of-charge back to the flight controller. This omission typically costs 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist** > - [x] **Momentum Theory** is the bedrock of all rotorcraft propulsion analysis. Memorize: $T = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (V_e^2 - V_0^2)$ > - [x] **Hover Induced Velocity**: $V_i = \sqrt{T / (2\rho A)}$ — this single equation appears in over 70% of propulsion problems. > - [x] **Ideal Hover Power**: $P = T^{3/2} / \sqrt{2 \rho A}$ — note the $T^{3/2}$ scaling. > - [x] **Thrust Coefficient**: $C_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$ — purely non-dimensional. > - [x] **Advance Ratio**: $J = V / (nD)$ — used only in forward flight, equals zero in pure hover. > - [x] **Motor KV**: $N_0 = KV \times V_{\text{batt}}$ — RPM at no load. > - [x] **Battery C-rating**: $I_{\max} = \text{C-rate} \times \text{Capacity (Ah)}$. > - [x] **LiPo Voltage**: 1 cell = 3.7 V nominal, 4.2 V full charge. 4S means 4 cells in series. > - [x] **Disk Area**: $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ — always compute this before any induced velocity calculation. > - [x] **Sea Level Air Density**: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — use unless otherwise stated. > - [x] **Real systems have Figure of Merit < 1**: actual power consumed is greater than ideal power. > - [x] **Always state assumptions** before any derivation (incompressible, inviscid, 1-D flow at disk). > - [x] **Propeller selection rule of thumb**: large diameter + low pitch for efficiency; small diameter + high pitch for agility. > - [x] **Tip speed limit**: avoid $V_{\text{tip}} > 0.7 \times$ speed of sound to prevent compressibility losses. > - [x] **Block diagrams win marks**: always draw a propulsion chain in long-answer questions.

topics.title: Payloads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT631 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Payloads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PAYLOAD (KTU 2024 Official Definition):**
> In aerial robotics, a **payload** is defined as the *mission-specific equipment, sensors, instruments, or cargo* carried by an Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) that is *not essential* for flight, but is essential for the *functional objective* of the mission. The payload is everything that lies **beyond** the airframe, propulsion, power, and flight-control subsystems.

From a systems engineering perspective, the payload can be formally expressed as:

$$M_{total} = M_{airframe} + M_{propulsion} + M_{avionics} + M_{battery} + M_{payload}$$

The **Payload Fraction ($\phi$)** is a critical KTU metric, defined as:

$$\phi = \frac{M_{payload}}{M_{total}}$$

A well-designed aerial robot typically maintains a payload fraction between **0.10 and 0.35** (10%–35%), depending on the vehicle class.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Delivery Truck" of the Sky

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Think of a delivery truck:**
> The truck's *engine, chassis, wheels, fuel tank, and steering* are like the **airframe and propulsion** of a UAV. The *packages in the back* are the **payload**. The truck driver does not get paid for the *truck itself* — they get paid for **safely delivering the packages (the payload)**. Similarly, an aerial robot's true value lies in what it *carries and executes*, not in flying.
> - **Heavy payload** = the truck uses more fuel, drives slower, and has less range. Same applies to a UAV.
> - **Bulky payload** = the truck needs a bigger cargo bay. The UAV needs bigger arms/mounts.
> - **Sensitive payload** (like a glass vase) = needs shock-absorbers. UAVs use **gimbals** and vibration isolators.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Payload Specification

Every payload, regardless of type, is characterized by **three fundamental KTU pillars**:

1. **Physical Pillar** — Mass, dimensions, mounting interface, Center of Gravity (CG) offset.
2. **Electrical Pillar** — Voltage, current draw, peak power, data interface (PWM, UART, USB, Ethernet, CAN).
3. **Environmental Pillar** — Operating temperature, humidity, IP rating, vibration tolerance.

## 1.4 The KTU 2024 Payload Classification Tree

| **Class** | **Sub-class** | **Example** | **Typical Mass** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | Optical | RGB Camera (Sony α7R IV) | 0.3 – 1.2 kg |
|  | Multispectral | MicaSense RedEdge | 0.15 – 0.3 kg |
|  | Hyperspectral | Headwall Nano-Hyperspec | 1.0 – 2.5 kg |
|  | Thermal (IR) | FLIR Tau 2 | 0.07 – 0.2 kg |
|  | LiDAR | Velodyne Puck / Ouster OS0 | 0.4 – 1.0 kg |
|  | Radar | mmWave 77 GHz (AWR1843) | 0.1 – 0.5 kg |
| **Communication** | Radio Relay | Doodle Labs Mesh Radio | 0.05 – 0.3 kg |
|  | SATCOM | Iridium 9602 | 0.25 – 0.5 kg |
| **Cargo / Delivery** | Package | Medical supplies, parcels | 0.5 – 25 kg |
| **Manipulator** | Gripper | 1-DOF gripper arm | 0.3 – 1.5 kg |
| **Combat / Kinetic** | Munition | (excluded from KTU scope) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 specifically focuses on the *classification, integration, and flight-performance impact* of payloads. Hardware-specific deep-dives (e.g., LiDAR scan patterns) are covered in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Payload Matters in Aerial Robotics — The Four Engineering Consequences

When a payload is added to a UAV, it triggers a **cascade of four engineering effects** that every KTU student must memorize:

### Effect 1 — Increased Gross Take-Off Weight (GTOW)
Higher weight demands **higher thrust** from every rotor. The new thrust requirement is:

$$T_{new} = (M_{empty} + M_{payload}) \cdot g$$

### Effect 2 — Reduced Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (T/W)
The **single most important** dynamic stability parameter in rotorcraft:

$$\left(\frac{T}{W}\right)_{new} = \frac{N \cdot T_{motor}}{M_{empty} \cdot g + M_{payload} \cdot g}$$

- A multirotor with `T/W ≥ 2.0` is **agile** (recommended for acrobatic flight).
- A multirotor with `T/W = 1.2 – 1.5` is **stable and efficient** (recommended for payload missions).
- A multirotor with `T/W < 1.0` **cannot fly**.

### Effect 3 — Reduced Endurance (Flight Time)
Endurance $E$ is governed by the **battery energy-to-power relationship**:

$$E_{endurance} = \frac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{batt}}{P_{hover} + P_{avionics} + P_{payload}}$$

where $P_{hover}$ itself grows with payload weight (discussed in SECTION 3).

### Effect 4 — Shift in Center of Gravity (CG)
A payload mounted **off-center** creates a **moment arm** $L$ that the flight controller must continuously counteract:

$$M_{disturbance} = M_{payload} \cdot g \cdot L$$

This is the *primary reason* gimbals and vibration-isolated mounts are mandatory in professional UAVs.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = \frac{M_{payload}}{M_{total}}$ | Payload fraction (dimensionless) | — |
| 2 | $\frac{T}{W} = \frac{N \cdot T_{motor}}{(M_{empty} + M_{payload}) \cdot g}$ | Thrust-to-weight ratio | — |
| 3 | $P_{hover} = \frac{(M g)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{disk}}}$ | Hover power (momentum theory) | W |
| 4 | $E = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{hover} + P_{avionics} + P_{payload}}$ | Endurance | s |
| 5 | $R = V_{cruise} \cdot E$ | Range | m |
| 6 | $M_{dist} = M_{payload} \cdot g \cdot L$ | Disturbance moment from off-axis payload | N·m |
| 7 | $\eta_{mission} = \frac{M_{payload}}{P_{total}}$ | Mission efficiency (kg per Watt) | kg/W |
| 8 | $C_{payload} = \frac{M_{payload}}{V_{payload}}$ | Payload density | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $I_{added} = M_{payload} \cdot r^{2}$ | Added moment of inertia (parallel axis) | kg·m² |
| 10 | $f_{vib} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{M_{total}}}$ | Vibration isolation natural frequency | Hz |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture (e.g., DJI Agras T40):** Multispectral + spraying payload ≈ 40 kg; forces T/W design at 1.4.
- **Disaster Response (e.g., Wingcopter 198):** Medical delivery payload 3 kg + insulation box; optimized for *range over speed*.
- **Mapping & Surveying (e.g., DJI M300 RTK + L1 LiDAR):** LiDAR payload weighs 0.9 kg; system uses 3-axis gimbal for sub-cm accuracy.
- **Defense ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance):** EO/IR turret + SIGINT payload; emphasizes *gimbal stabilization* and *encrypted datalinks*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters to a KTU examiner:** A "perfect" answer always links a *formula* to a *design trade-off*. Never state a formula without explaining what compromises it captures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DERIVATION 1 — THE HOVER POWER vs. PAYLOAD RELATIONSHIP**
> *This is the most-tested derivation in KTU ESE for Module 1.*

## 3.1 Deriving $P_{hover}$ as a Function of Payload Mass

**Starting point — Actuator Disk Theory (Momentum Theory):**
A hovering rotor accelerates air downward through a disk of area $A_{disk}$, creating a uniform velocity $v_i$ (induced velocity) at the disk.

**Step 1 — Force balance across the disk:**
The thrust generated by a single rotor equals the rate of change of momentum of the air mass flowing through it.

$$T = \dot{m} \cdot v_i = (\rho A_{disk} v_i) \cdot v_i = \rho A_{disk} v_i^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the induced velocity in terms of thrust:**
Rearranging the equation above:

$$v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho A_{disk}}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of energy conservation (kinetic energy imparted to the air):**
The power required to maintain hover is the thrust multiplied by the induced velocity:

$$P_{hover} = T \cdot v_i$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the induced velocity expression:**

$$P_{hover} = T \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho A_{disk}}} = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\rho A_{disk}}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the thrust expressed in terms of total weight $W = T$ for hover:**

$$P_{hover} = \frac{(M g)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{\rho A_{disk}}} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

(The factor of $\sqrt{2}$ appears when we account for both the far-wake velocity and the disk velocity; the rigorous derivation is in Leishman’s *Principles of Helicopter Aerodynamics*.)

$$\boxed{\,P_{hover} = \frac{(M_{total} \cdot g)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{disk}}}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Expand $M_{total} = M_{empty} + M_{payload}$ to see the payload impact explicitly:**

$$P_{hover} = \frac{g^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{disk}}} \cdot (M_{empty} + M_{payload})^{3/2}$$

**Key physical insight:** Because of the **3/2 power law**, **doubling the payload** does **not** double the hover power — it increases it by a factor of $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A quadcopter has an empty mass of **1.5 kg**, a payload of **0.5 kg**, four rotors each with disk area $A_{disk} = 0.0785 \text{ m}^2$ (15 cm radius propellers), and operates at sea level ($\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Calculate (a) hover power, (b) endurance for a 6S 5000 mAh LiPo (22.2 V, 150 Wh), assuming avionics draw 5 W and payload draws 8 W. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

### Part (a) — Hover Power

**Step 1 — Total mass:**

$$M_{total} = M_{empty} + M_{payload} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total weight:**

$$W = M_{total} \cdot g = 2.0 \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the hover power formula:**

$$P_{hover} = \frac{(W)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{disk}}}$$

$$P_{hover} = \frac{(19.62)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.0785}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$(19.62)^{3/2} = 19.62 \times \sqrt{19.62} = 19.62 \times 4.4295 = 86.91 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:**

$$\sqrt{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.0785} = \sqrt{0.1923} = 0.4385 \text{ (units: }\sqrt{\text{W·s/m}}\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Final hover power per rotor:**

$$P_{hover,1} = \frac{86.91}{0.4385} = 198.2 \text{ W per rotor}$$

**Step 7 — Total hover power (4 rotors, ideal):**

$$P_{hover,total} = 4 \times 198.2 = 792.8 \text{ W (ideal momentum-theory)}$$ 

In reality (Figure-of-Merit ≈ 0.6), the actual power is:

$$P_{actual} = \frac{792.8}{0.6} \approx 1321 \text{ W}$$

### Part (b) — Endurance

$$P_{total} = P_{hover,actual} + P_{avionics} + P_{payload} = 1321 + 5 + 8 = 1334 \text{ W}$$

$$E = \frac{150 \text{ Wh}}{1334 \text{ W}} = 0.1125 \text{ h} = 6.75 \text{ min}$$

---

## 3.3 The Payload Endurance Sensitivity Equation

**Derivation of the sensitivity of endurance to payload mass:**

Starting from the basic endurance equation, differentiate with respect to $M_{payload}$:

$$\frac{dE}{dM_{payload}} = -\frac{C_{batt} V_{batt} \cdot \frac{3}{2} \cdot g \cdot (M_{empty} + M_{payload})^{1/2}}{\left[(M_{empty} + M_{payload})^{3/2} \cdot g^{3/2} / \sqrt{2\rho A} + P_{aux}\right]^2}$$

The negative sign confirms that **endurance strictly decreases as payload increases**, and the **non-linear** dependence means small payload additions near the maximum take-off weight cause *disproportionately large* endurance losses.

$$\boxed{\,\Delta E \approx -\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{E_0 \cdot \Delta M_{payload}}{M_{empty} + M_{payload,0}}\,}$$

This equation lets KTU students estimate the *endurance penalty* of adding a new sensor without re-deriving from scratch.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — UAV Payload Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AIR[UAV AIRFRAME SUBSYSTEM]
        A1[Frame and Chassis]
        A2[Propulsion Motors and ESCs]
        A3[Flight Controller Pixhawk]
        A4[Power Distribution Board]
        A5[LiPo Battery Pack]
    end

    subgraph PAY[PAYLOAD SUBSYSTEM]
        P1[Mission Sensor Camera LiDAR Multispectral]
        P2[Onboard Compute Companion Computer]
        P3[Gimbal Stabilizer 3 axis]
        P4[Data Link Telemetry Radio]
        P5[Vibration Isolation Mount]
    end

    subgraph GCS[GROUND CONTROL STATION]
        G1[Operator Console Laptop Tablet]
        G2[Mission Planner QGroundControl]
        G3[Data Processing Server]
    end

    A5 --> A4
    A4 --> A2
    A3 --> A2
    A5 --> P1
    A5 --> P2
    A3 --> P3
    P5 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> P2
    A3 -.MAVLink Protocol.-> G1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flow — Payload Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the mission objective?}
    Q1 -->|Imaging| S1[Optical or Thermal Camera]
    Q1 -->|Mapping| S2[LiDAR with IMU]
    Q1 -->|Agriculture| S3[Multispectral Sensor]
    Q1 -->|Delivery| S4[Cargo Container with Release Mechanism]
    Q1 -->|Comms Relay| S5[Radio Repeater]

    S1 --> W1{Payload mass check}
    S2 --> W1
    S3 --> W1
    S4 --> W1
    S5 --> W1

    W1 -->|Within limits| OK[Flight Authorized]
    W1 -->|Exceeds T and W| NO[Redesign Airframe or Reduce Payload]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow from Battery through Payload

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[LiPo Battery 22.2 V] --> PDB[Power Distribution Board]
    PDB --> M1[Motor 1 ESC]
    PDB --> M2[Motor 2 ESC]
    PDB --> M3[Motor 3 ESC]
    PDB --> M4[Motor 4 ESC]
    PDB --> FC[Flight Controller 5 V BEC]
    PDB --> PAY[Payload 12 V or 5 V Buck Converter]
    FC --> SENS[IMU GPS Compass]
    PAY --> CAM[Camera or Sensor]
    PAY --> GIM[Gimbal 12 V]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In KTU board exams, drawing a *functional block diagram* (even in rough form) earns easy partial marks, especially when the question asks "explain the integration of payload with the UAV."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define payload in the context of aerial robotics. List any four types of payloads with one real-world example each.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A payload is the mission-specific equipment carried by a UAV that is not essential for flight but is essential for the mission objective. Four types:
1. **Imaging payload** — e.g., Sony α7R IV RGB camera for aerial photography.
2. **LiDAR payload** — e.g., Velodyne Puck for 3-D mapping.
3. **Multispectral payload** — e.g., MicaSense RedEdge for precision agriculture.
4. **Cargo payload** — e.g., medical supplies in a Wingcopter 198 delivery drone.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`
**What is payload fraction? Why is it an important design parameter for UAVs?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Payload fraction is the ratio of payload mass to total take-off mass:

$$\phi = \frac{M_{payload}}{M_{total}}$$

It is important because:
1. It directly governs the **thrust-to-weight ratio**, which determines agility and stability.
2. A low payload fraction means the airframe is *over-engineered* and the mission is *cost-inefficient*.
3. A high payload fraction risks **structural failure, reduced endurance, and CG instability**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the **hover power of a rotorcraft** in terms of total mass, using the actuator-disk momentum theory. Show clearly the assumption of uniform induced velocity. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A hexacopter has the following specifications: empty mass = 4 kg, payload = 2 kg, six rotors each of disk radius = 12 cm, sea-level density = 1.225 kg/m³, Figure of Merit = 0.65. Calculate: (i) the **ideal hover power per rotor**, (ii) the **actual total hover power**, and (iii) the **payload fraction**. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
*(See SECTION 3.1, Steps 1–6 for the full derivation. The student should reproduce: $T = \rho A v_i^2$, $v_i = \sqrt{T/\rho A}$, $P = T \cdot v_i$, leading to $P_{hover} = (Mg)^{3/2}/\sqrt{2\rho A}$.)*

**Assumption to state explicitly:** *Uniform induced velocity $v_i$ across the entire rotor disk (no swirl, no tip losses).*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Ideal hover power per rotor:**

$$M_{total} = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ kg}, \quad W = 58.86 \text{ N}$$
$$A_{disk} = \pi (0.12)^2 = 0.04524 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{hover,1} = \frac{(58.86)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.04524}}$$

$$(58.86)^{3/2} = 58.86 \times 7.672 = 451.6 \text{ W}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.04524} = \sqrt{0.1108} = 0.3329$$

$$P_{hover,1} = \frac{451.6}{0.3329} = 1356.5 \text{ W per rotor (ideal)}$$

**(ii) Actual total hover power:**

$$P_{hover,total} = 6 \times 1356.5 \times \frac{1}{0.65} = 8139 / 0.65 = 12522 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Payload fraction:**

$$\phi = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333 = 33.3\%$$

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**(a)** Explain the **four engineering consequences** of adding a payload to a multirotor UAV. For each consequence, write the governing equation and state its design implication. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **types of sensor payloads** (optical, thermal, LiDAR, multispectral, hyperspectral) used in aerial robotics. For each, state one application, the typical mass range, and the data interface. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| **Consequence** | **Equation** | **Design Implication** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Increased GTOW | $W = (M_e + M_p) g$ | Stronger frame, higher-class motors needed |
| 2. Reduced T/W | $T/W = \frac{N T_{motor}}{(M_e + M_p) g}$ | Less agility; for stability keep $T/W \geq 1.4$ |
| 3. Reduced Endurance | $E = E_{batt}/P_{hover} \propto (M_e + M_p)^{-3/2}$ | Mission radius shrinks; battery upgrade needed |
| 4. CG Shift | $M_{dist} = M_p g L$ | Gimbal & active balancing required |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Sensor Payload Table

| **Type** | **Application** | **Mass** | **Interface** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Optical (RGB) | Inspection, mapping | 0.3–1.2 kg | USB 3, Ethernet |
| Thermal (IR) | Search & rescue, solar panel inspection | 0.07–0.2 kg | USB, analog video |
| LiDAR | 3-D mapping, forestry | 0.4–1.0 kg | Ethernet, UDP |
| Multispectral | Crop health (NDVI) | 0.15–0.3 kg | UART, USB |
| Hyperspectral | Mineral exploration, precision agri | 1.0–2.5 kg | USB 3, SATA |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP REASONS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS TOPIC:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor** in the hover power derivation — it is the most-skipped step and costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up $M_{total}$ with $M_{empty}$** in the T/W ratio — the payload mass *must* be added.
> 3. **Not stating the assumption** of *uniform induced velocity* in the momentum theory — KTU examiners explicitly check this and deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "more payload = less flight time"** without the formula — always back qualitative statements with the **3/2 power law** equation.
> 5. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — KTU board rules say: *no unit = no mark* for that quantity.
> 6. **Confusing "payload" with "load"** — payload is *mission equipment*; load can include fuel. Be precise in definitions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MASTER THESE BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL**

- [ ] **Definition:** Payload = mission-specific equipment, *not* required for flight.
- [ ] **Payload fraction $\phi$** is the single most-quoted KTU metric. Always compute and quote it.
- [ ] **Hover power** scales as $M^{3/2}$ — non-linear, *not* linear.
- [ ] **T/W ratio** must stay $\geq 1.2$ for stable payload flight, $\geq 2.0$ for agility.
- [ ] **CG shift** causes disturbance moments — gimbals and active balancing are mandatory.
- [ ] **Three pillars of payload spec:** Physical, Electrical, Environmental.
- [ ] **Five major sensor types:** Optical, Thermal, LiDAR, Multispectral, Hyperspectral.
- [ ] **Two non-sensor classes:** Communication relay and Cargo delivery.
- [ ] **Endurance sensitivity** equation: $\Delta E \approx -\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{E_0 \cdot \Delta M_p}{M_e + M_{p,0}}$.
- [ ] **Always** state the *uniform induced velocity* assumption before any momentum-theory derivation.
- [ ] **Always** include *units* in every numerical step.
- [ ] **Figure of Merit (FM)** accounts for real-world losses — typically 0.5–0.7 for small multirotors.
- [ ] **Power flow architecture:** Battery → PDB → (Motors + Avionics + Payload) — know this diagram cold.
- [ ] **Integration trade-off triangle:** *Range, Endurance, Payload* — you can only optimize two of the three.
- [ ] **Application memory anchors:** Agriculture = multispectral, Mapping = LiDAR, Delivery = cargo, SAR = thermal, Inspection = RGB.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Launch and Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT631 - AERIAL ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Launch and Recovery # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Launch** is the controlled initiation of an Unmanned Aerial Vehicle's (UAV) flight regime, transitioning the airframe from a ground/static state to a sustained, stable airborne state by providing sufficient kinetic and potential energy to overcome stall and establish positive climb. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recovery** is the controlled termination of a UAV's flight regime, dissipating its kinetic and potential energy through a pre-planned sequence (approach, flare, touchdown, or capture) such that the airframe is safely returned to a reusable condition with no human occupant risk. In the context of **Aerial Robotics**, launch and recovery are *unmanned equivalents* of manned aviation's takeoff and landing phases, but they are uniquely challenging because **no pilot is onboard to react to real-time anomalies**. Therefore, every parameter — launch velocity, climb gradient, approach speed, sink rate, flare altitude — must be **pre-programmed, autonomous, and fault-tolerant**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **shuttlecock (badminton birdie)** being served and then falling back into a player's hand. > - **Launch** is analogous to the **server's racket strike**: a single impulsive energy transfer (hand-launch, bungee, or catapult) imparts the velocity needed for the shuttle to clear the net. > - **Recovery** is analogous to the **return trajectory** before the player catches it: the shuttle follows a stable glide path, bleeding energy through aerodynamic drag, until it is intercepted. > > Just as the shuttlecock has *no internal propulsion* (relying only on its launch impulse), many small fixed-wing UAVs (Class I and Class II) are *impulse-launched* and *unpowered-recovered*. Heavier or longer-endurance platforms behave more like **paper airplanes** — sustained glides requiring precise energy management. ## 1.3 Why Launch & Recovery Is a Standalone Discipline in Aerial Robotics | Aspect | Manned Aviation | Aerial Robotics (UAV) | |---|---|---| | Decision maker in cockpit | Human pilot | Autopilot / Ground pilot | | Tolerance for hard impact | High (with ejection seats) | Limited (avionics, payload) | | Launch energy source | Engines (self-propelled) | Often impulse (catapult, hand) | | Recovery location | Prepared runways | Varied: belly, net, parachute, ship, VTOL | | Repeatability demand | Per-flight | Per-flight, often **high-frequency** | > [!NOTE] > In many KTU problems, the launch and recovery system is the **single largest mass and volume contributor** in a small UAV, often **30%–50%** of the overall system weight. Designers therefore must select a launch/recovery method that minimizes parasitic mass while satisfying mission endurance. ## 1.4 Classification of Launch and Recovery Methods ``` LAUNCH METHODS RECOVERY METHODS ───────────── ──────────────── • Hand launch • Belly/ground slide landing • Bungee / Catapult • Net capture • Rail launch • Parachute descent • Runway / wheeled takeoff • Runway landing (gear) • VTOL (vertical takeoff) • Deep-stall / Arrested landing • Pneumatic / hydraulic launch • VTOL recovery (hover touchdown) • Shipboard catapult (naval UAV) • Shipboard net (RQ-2 Pioneer style) • Mother-ship air-drop • Airbag / crushable structure • Mid-air retrieval (rare) ``` ## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following numerical benchmarks are the **industry-standard reference values** examiners expect you to memorize: > > - **Standard sea-level air density** $\rho = \mathbf{1.225 \ kg/m^3}$ > - **Gravitational acceleration** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$ > - **Typical small UAV stall speed** $V_{stall} = \mathbf{15 - 25 \ m/s}$ > - **Typical launch impulse duration** $t_{launch} = \mathbf{0.1 - 0.5 \ s}$ > - **Acceptable landing sink rate** $\dot{h}_{max} = \mathbf{1.0 - 2.5 \ m/s}$ (varies with airframe robustness) > - **Bungee energy storage** $E_{bungee} = \mathbf{0.5 \, k \, x^2}$ where $k$ is elastic constant, $x$ is stretch # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Launch Phase — Operational Logic Decomposition The launch phase can be broken into **four sequential sub-phases**: 1. **Pre-launch (T-0):** Airframe held static; launcher (catapult/rail/hand) loaded with stored energy. 2. **Impulse Phase:** Stored energy is rapidly converted to kinetic energy of the airframe. Duration typically **0.1 s – 0.5 s**. 3. **Transition Phase:** Airframe separates from launcher at or above $V_{stall}$; control surfaces become effective. 4. **Climb-out:** Sustained climb under propulsion or unpowered glide until mission altitude is reached. > [!NOTE] > **Why does it matter?** If the launch impulse ends *below* $V_{stall}$, the UAV **cannot generate enough lift** and will drop. The catapult/launch system must therefore deliver **at minimum** the kinetic energy corresponding to $V_{stall}$ at the moment of release. ## 2.2 Detailed Launch Methods — Engineering Logic ### A. Hand Launch - Operator provides initial kinetic energy via arm throw. - $V_0 \approx 8 - 12 \ m/s$ typical. - Only viable for very light UAVs ($< 5 \ kg$). - **Failure mode:** Operator fatigue, misjudged release attitude → stall. ### B. Bungee / Elastic Catapult - A stretched elastomer (bungee cord) stores **elastic potential energy** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$. - On release, this energy accelerates the UAV along a guide rail. - Commonly used for small fixed-wing UAVs up to **25 kg** (e.g., RQ-11 Raven uses bungee). ### C. Pneumatic / Hydraulic Catapult - Compressed air or hydraulic ram drives a shuttle (to which UAV is attached). - Used for **heavier UAVs** (e.g., Insitu ScanEagle weighs ~15 kg and uses a pneumatic launcher; Naval UAVs like the Boeing Insitu Integrator use hydraulic catapults for heavier platforms). - Energy delivered: $E = p \cdot A \cdot L$ (pressure × piston area × stroke). ### D. Runway Takeoff (Self-powered) - Conventional rolling takeoff with landing gear. - Required when UAV is too heavy for impulse launch or when propulsion is insufficient at low speeds. - Runway length requirement given by FAR-style ground run equation. ### E. VTOL Launch - Direct transition from hover to forward flight. - Used by quadcopters, tilt-rotors, tailsitters. - Energy supplied entirely by propulsion system — no external launcher needed. ### F. Mother-ship Air-Drop - UAV stowed inside a larger aircraft (e.g., mothership) and released at mission altitude. - The DARPA Gremlins program and USAF C-130-launched switches exemplify this. ## 2.3 The Recovery Phase — Operational Logic Decomposition Recovery is **energetically the mirror of launch**: the UAV must bleed its kinetic and potential energy in a controlled, predictable way. Five phases: 1. **Mission termination:** UAV leaves mission area and turns toward recovery zone. 2. **Approach:** Stabilized descent on glide slope, typically 3°–7° below horizontal. 3. **Flare:** Pitch-up maneuver to reduce sink rate and airspeed before touchdown. 4. **Touchdown / Capture:** Energy dissipated via friction, net, parachute, or landing gear. 5. **Post-touchdown:** Aircraft decelerates and is secured. ## 2.4 Detailed Recovery Methods — Engineering Logic ### A. Belly Landing (Skid Landing) - UAV has no landing gear; fuselage bottom contacts ground. - Simplest, lightest, lowest cost. - Acceptable for UAVs designed for **single mission or limited re-use**. - Sink rate must be limited ($< 1.5 \ m/s$ typical). ### B. Net Recovery - UAV flies into a suspended net at end of mission. - Net absorbs kinetic energy via fabric stretching and frame deformation. - Used for shipboard (ScanEagle), confined-area operations, and end-of-line parachute-aided recovery. ### C. Parachute Recovery - Parachute deployed (often at mission end or in emergency). - Terminal descent velocity $V_t$ determined by drag-weight balance: $$\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V_t^2 = mg$$ - For a 5 kg UAV with 1 m² canopy and $C_D \approx 1.5$: $V_t \approx 5.2 \ m/s$. ### D. Runway / Conventional Landing - Uses retractable landing gear; controlled flare and rollout. - Highest recovery complexity; used by large UAVs (e.g., MQ-9 Reaper). ### E. Deep-Stall Landing - UAV deliberately flown beyond stall angle of attack; high drag decelerates the airframe rapidly. - Requires **zero sink rate at touchdown** — extremely precise. - Used in concept designs and some specialized UAVs. ### F. VTOL Recovery (Vertical Landing) - UAV transitions from forward flight to hover, then descends vertically. - Used by quadcopters, tilt-rotors (e.g., Bell V-247), tailsitters. - Demands **real-time altitude + position sensing** (GPS, lidar, barometer). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the exam-ready formula bank for Launch & Recovery problems. Memorize these before walking into the KTU exam hall.** | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Required launch kinetic energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m V_{stall}^2$ | $m$: kg, $V_{stall}$: m/s, $E_k$: J | | 2 | Bungee stored elastic energy | $E_b = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | $k$: N/m, $x$: m | | 3 | Bungee launch velocity | $V_0 = x \sqrt{k/m}$ | assumes $100\%$ energy transfer | | 4 | Catapult work done | $W = F \cdot d$ | $F$: N, $d$: stroke length (m) | | 5 | Hydraulic/pneumatic energy | $E = p \cdot A \cdot L$ | $p$: Pa, $A$: m², $L$: m | | 6 | Acceleration during launch | $a = \frac{V_0^2}{2 d}$ | from kinematics $V_0^2 = 2 a d$ | | 7 | Launch g-load | $n = a/g$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Parachute terminal velocity | $V_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m g}{\rho C_D A}}$ | $A$: canopy area (m²) | | 9 | Glide ratio | $G = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Glide range | $R = h \cdot (L/D)$ | $h$: release altitude (m) | | 11 | Sink rate in glide | $\dot{h} = V \cdot (D/L)$ | m/s | | 12 | Approach speed | $V_{app} = 1.3 \cdot V_{stall}$ | industry rule-of-thumb | | 13 | Stall speed (level flight) | $V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 W}{\rho S C_{L,max}}}$ | $S$: wing area, $C_{L,max}$ | | 14 | Ground run distance (rough) | $s_g = \dfrac{1.44 \, W^2}{g \, \rho \, S \, C_{L,max} \, \mu \, T}$ | simplified; $\mu$: friction | | 15 | Touchdown kinetic energy | $E_{td} = \tfrac{1}{2} m V_{td}^2 + m g h_{flare}$ | flare height $h_{flare}$ | | 16 | Net capture impulse force | $F_{net} = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$ | $\Delta p = m V_{app}$ | | 17 | Shipboard landing sink rate limit | $\dot{h} \leq 1.5 \ m/s$ | for ship-deployed UAVs | | 18 | Energy dissipation by skid | $E_{fric} = \mu \, m g \, d_{slide}$ | $d_{slide}$: slide distance | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the formula sheet, the most-tested formulas are #3 (bungee launch velocity), #8 (parachute terminal velocity), #11 (sink rate in glide), and #13 (stall speed). Master these four, and you cover ~70% of likely numerical problems. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Military reconnaissance (e.g., IAI Heron, RQ-20 Puma):** use pneumatic catapult and net/skid recovery for shipboard and forward-base operations. - **Agriculture (e.g., senseFly eBee):** hand-launched, belly-landed — minimizes payload system mass. - **Delivery drones (Amazon Prime Air prototype):** VTOL for both launch and recovery. - **Disaster response:** parachute recovery ensures UAV can be retrieved from inaccessible terrain. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bungee Launch Velocity **Problem context:** A UAV of mass $m$ is launched using a bungee cord of spring constant $k$ stretched by length $x$. Assuming **100% energy transfer** (no friction, no air drag), derive the launch velocity at the moment of release. **Step 1 — Identify the stored energy at full stretch.** The bungee cord is a linear elastic element obeying Hooke's Law. The elastic potential energy stored when stretched by $x$ is: $$E_{elastic} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$ where $k$ is in $\text{N/m}$ and $x$ in meters. **Step 2 — Identify the kinetic energy at release.** At the moment the UAV leaves the rail (or operator's hand), all the bungee's stored energy has been converted into the kinetic energy of the airframe: $$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m V_0^2$$ **Step 3 — Apply conservation of energy.** By the conservation of mechanical energy, and given our **no-loss** assumption: $$\frac{1}{2} m V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$ **Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $V_0$.** Cancel $\tfrac{1}{2}$ on both sides: $$m V_0^2 = k x^2$$ Divide by $m$: $$V_0^2 = \frac{k x^2}{m}$$ Take the square root of both sides: $$V_0 = x \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ **Step 5 — Final boxed result.** $$\boxed{V_0 = x \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}}$$ **Step 6 — Sanity check (units).** Inside the square root: $\dfrac{\text{N/m}}{\text{kg}} = \dfrac{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2 / \text{m}}{\text{kg}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{s}^2}$. Square root gives $1/\text{s}$, multiplied by meters gives $\text{m/s}$. ✓ Units correct. **Step 7 — Numerical example.** Given $m = 4 \ \text{kg}$, $k = 800 \ \text{N/m}$, $x = 2 \ \text{m}$: $$V_0 = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{800}{4}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{200} = 2 \cdot 14.142 = 28.28 \ \text{m/s}$$ This comfortably exceeds a typical $V_{stall} \approx 15 \ \text{m/s}$ for such a UAV. **Launch is feasible.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parachute Terminal Velocity **Problem context:** A UAV of mass $m$ descends under a circular parachute of canopy area $A$ and drag coefficient $C_D$. Determine the constant (terminal) descent speed. **Step 1 — Free body diagram at terminal velocity.** At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero. Two forces act vertically: - Weight downward: $W = m g$ - Aerodynamic drag upward: $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V_t^2$ **Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law (equilibrium).** $$\sum F_z = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V_t^2 - m g = 0$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange.** $$\frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V_t^2 = m g$$ $$V_t^2 = \frac{2 m g}{\rho C_D A}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $V_t$ (positive root).** $$\boxed{V_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m g}{\rho C_D A}}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical example.** Given $m = 5 \ \text{kg}$, $A = 1.0 \ \text{m}^2$, $C_D = 1.5$, $\rho = 1.225 \ \text{kg/m}^3$: $$V_t = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 5 \cdot 9.81}{1.225 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 1.0}} = \sqrt{\frac{98.1}{1.8375}} = \sqrt{53.39} \approx 7.31 \ \text{m/s}$$ This is *high* — above the typical limit. Real UAV parachutes use larger canopies or reefing mechanisms. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sink Rate in a Steady Glide **Problem context:** A UAV in unpowered glide has lift $L$ balancing weight and drag $D$ opposing motion. Derive the sink rate as a function of airspeed and lift-to-drag ratio. **Step 1 — Force balance in steady glide.** For a straight, steady (non-accelerated) glide at small flight-path angle $\gamma$: $$L = W \cos\gamma \approx W \quad \text{(small-angle)}$$ $$D = W \sin\gamma \approx W \gamma$$ **Step 2 — Define glide ratio.** The glide ratio is the ratio of horizontal distance to vertical distance lost: $$G = \frac{L}{D} = \frac{1}{\tan\gamma} \approx \frac{1}{\gamma}$$ **Step 3 — Express $\gamma$ from glide ratio.** $$\tan\gamma = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{1}{G}$$ **Step 4 — Vertical velocity component (sink rate).** Vertical velocity is $V \sin\gamma \approx V \tan\gamma$ for small angles: $$\dot{h} = V \sin\gamma \approx V \tan\gamma = V \cdot \frac{D}{L}$$ **Step 5 — Final result.** $$\boxed{\dot{h} = \dfrac{V}{G} = V \cdot \dfrac{D}{L}}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical example.** A UAV at $V = 20 \ \text{m/s}$ with glide ratio $L/D = 10$: $$\dot{h} = \frac{20}{10} = 2.0 \ \text{m/s}$$ This is **at the upper edge** of acceptable landing sink rate. The glide must be **flared** before touchdown. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Required Launch Acceleration & g-Load **Problem context:** A catapult accelerates a UAV from rest to launch speed $V_0$ over a stroke length $d$. Find the launch acceleration and the structural g-load. **Step 1 — Kinematic equation (constant acceleration).** $$V_0^2 = u^2 + 2 a d$$ With $u = 0$: $$V_0^2 = 2 a d$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $a$.** $$\boxed{a = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 d}}$$ **Step 3 — Express in terms of g.** $$\boxed{n = \dfrac{a}{g} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 d g}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical example.** $V_0 = 20 \ \text{m/s}$, $d = 1.0 \ \text{m}$: $$a = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \ \text{m/s}^2 \approx 20.4 \ g$$ This is **high** — most airframes tolerate only **6–10 g** during launch. Hence impulse launchers require either longer stroke or UAVs with low $V_{stall}$. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Decision Chart > [!NOTE] > **This matrix is what a KTU examiner expects you to reproduce when asked "compare launch methods."** | Method | Typical Mass Range | Recovery Complexity | Cost | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---| | Hand launch | 0.5 – 5 kg | Low | Very low | Tactical micro-UAVs | | Bungee | 1 – 25 kg | Low–Medium | Low | Field-deployable small UAVs | | Pneumatic catapult | 5 – 50 kg | Medium | Medium | Shipboard / expeditionary | | Runway | > 50 kg | High | High | Medium-altitude long-endurance | | VTOL | 0.1 – 500 kg | Medium | Variable | Multi-rotor urban delivery | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Launch & Recovery Phase Sequence — Top-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[UAV Static on Launcher] --> B[Pre-launch Check
Energy Loaded] B --> C[Impulse Phase
0.1-0.5 s] C --> D{Above Vstall?} D -- No --> E[Launch Failure
Stall and Drop] D -- Yes --> F[Transition Phase
Control Surfaces Effective] F --> G[Climb-out to Mission Altitude] G --> H[Mission Phase] H --> I[Recovery Commanded] I --> J[Approach Phase
Stabilized Descent] J --> K{Recovery Method} K -- Belly --> L[Touchdown on Skids] K -- Net --> M[Capture in Suspended Net] K -- Parachute --> N[Parachute Deployed
Drag Decelerates] K -- VTOL --> O[Transition to Hover
Vertical Touchdown] K -- Runway --> P[Flare and Rollout] L --> Q[UAV Secured] M --> Q N --> Q O --> Q P --> Q ``` ## 4.2 Bungee Launch System — Block Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PreLaunch[Pre-launch State] P1[Stretched Bungee
Stores 0.5 kx squared] --> P2[UAV Latched to Rail] end P2 --> R1[Release Trigger] R1 --> R2[Elastic Energy Released] R2 --> R3[UAV Accelerates along Rail] R3 --> R4[Speed Crosses Vstall] R4 --> R5[UAV Separates from Rail] R5 --> R6[Autopilot Engages
Pitch-up to Climb] ``` ## 4.3 Parachute Recovery — Functional Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Recovery Command Issued] --> B[Flight Computer Initiates Recovery] B --> C{Recovery Type} C -- Normal --> D[Pre-programmed Glide to LZ] C -- Emergency --> E[Immediate Parachute Deploy] D --> F[Approach to LZ
Glide Ratio L slash D] F --> G[Over LZ: Pyro Fires] E --> H[Pyro Fires Immediately] G --> I[Drogue Chute First
Stabilizes Descent] H --> I I --> J[Main Chute Deploys
Drag: 0.5 rho CD A V squared] J --> K[Terminal Velocity Reached
Vt = sqrt 2mg over rho CDA] K --> L[Ground Impact
Acceptable sink rate] ``` ## 4.4 Launch Method Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Design Question
How to Launch?] --> B{Mass m} B -- under 5 kg --> C{Endurance} B -- 5 to 25 kg --> D[Consider Bungee or Pneumatic] B -- above 25 kg --> E[Runway or VTOL] C -- Short --> F[Hand Launch] C -- Long --> G[Pneumatic Catapult] E --> H{Operational Area} H -- Open field --> I[Runway Wheels] H -- Urban / Confined --> J[VTOL] ``` ## 4.5 Recovery Method Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Design Question
How to Recover?] --> B{Landing Zone} B -- Shipboard --> C[Net Capture] B -- Open ground --> D{Reusability} B -- Urban --> E[VTOL or Parachute] D -- Single use --> F[Belly or Parachute] D -- Reusable --> G{Wheel Available?} G -- Yes --> H[Runway Landing] G -- No --> I[Skid or Parachute] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Each question is modeled exactly on KTU's Part A format: short, direct, and answerable in **2–4 sentences**. --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is impulse launching? Name two UAV systems that use this technique.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Impulse launching is a method of initiating UAV flight in which a **pre-stored energy source** (elastic, pneumatic, or human) is rapidly released to accelerate the airframe to a speed at or above its stall velocity within a very short time (typically 0.1–0.5 s), after which the UAV sustains flight under its own aerodynamic lift. Two systems that use impulse launching: 1. **RQ-11 Raven** — uses a bungee cord launcher. 2. **Boeing Insitu ScanEagle** — uses a pneumatic catapult (SkyHook or equivalent). The key advantage of impulse launching is that it eliminates the need for a runway and onboard acceleration energy, making the UAV **lightweight and expeditionary**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Distinguish between belly landing and net recovery of a UAV. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Belly Landing | Net Recovery | |---|---|---| | Mechanism | Fuselage contacts ground directly | UAV flies into a suspended net | | Mass penalty | **None** (no gear) | Net frame carried or installed at LZ | | Reusability | Limited | Higher (low impact loads) | **Belly landing advantage:** Minimum structural weight; simplest design. **Belly landing disadvantage:** High wear on fuselage; not suitable for heavy or sensitive payloads. **Net recovery advantage:** Permits recovery in confined/shipboard spaces; protects avionics from impact. **Net recovery disadvantage:** Requires precise approach guidance; net infrastructure must be deployed. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Two complete alternative choices are given below. --- ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a free-body diagram, derive the expression for the **terminal velocity of a UAV descending under a parachute**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 6 kg UAV is fitted with a circular parachute of diameter 1.5 m and drag coefficient $C_D = 1.4$. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate: **(i)** the terminal descent velocity, and **(ii)** the descent time from an altitude of 500 m if terminal velocity is reached almost immediately. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** *Assumptions:* (1) Steady descent, (2) Vertical orientation of canopy, (3) Standard sea-level air density, (4) Parachute drag coefficient is constant. Free-body diagram: - Weight $W = mg$ acting downward. - Aerodynamic drag $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2$ acting upward. At terminal velocity, acceleration = 0: $$\frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V_t^2 = m g$$ $$V_t^2 = \frac{2 m g}{\rho C_D A}$$ $$V_t = \sqrt{\frac{2 m g}{\rho C_D A}}$$ (Marks: 1 for diagram, 2 for equation setup, 2 for derivation, 2 for clarity & assumptions) --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Terminal velocity:** Diameter $D = 1.5 \ m$ → Area $A = \tfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(1.5)^2 = 1.767 \ m^2$. $$V_t = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6 \times 9.81}{1.225 \times 1.4 \times 1.767}}$$ $$V_t = \sqrt{\frac{117.72}{3.0302}} = \sqrt{38.85} \approx 6.23 \ \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Descent time from 500 m:** Assuming constant terminal velocity: $$t = \frac{h}{V_t} = \frac{500}{6.23} \approx 80.26 \ \text{s}$$ (Marks: 1 for area calc, 2 for $V_t$ substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 2 for $t$ calc) --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]** **(a)** A UAV of mass 4 kg is launched using a bungee cord with spring constant $k = 600 \ \text{N/m}$, stretched by $x = 2.5 \ \text{m}$. Assuming $100\%$ energy transfer, derive and calculate the **launch velocity** at the moment of release. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List and briefly explain **four different recovery methods** used for unmanned aerial vehicles. For each method, state one advantage and one typical application. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Bungee launch velocity (7 marks):** Conservation of energy (elastic PE = kinetic KE): $$\frac{1}{2} k x^2 = \frac{1}{2} m V_0^2$$ $$V_0^2 = \frac{k x^2}{m}$$ $$V_0 = x \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ Substituting values: $$V_0 = 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{600}{4}} = 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{150} = 2.5 \cdot 12.247 \approx 30.62 \ \text{m/s}$$ This value must exceed the UAV's stall speed. For a 4 kg UAV, $V_{stall} \approx 15 \ m/s$ — comfortably exceeded. (Marks: 1 for energy equation, 2 for derivation, 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic + interpretation) --- **Part (b) — Four recovery methods (7 marks):** 1. **Belly Landing (3 marks distributed):** - Airframe bottom contacts ground without landing gear. - *Advantage:* Lowest weight and complexity. - *Application:* Tactical micro-UAVs (e.g., AeroVironment Switchblade-class or hand-launched ISR drones). 2. **Parachute Recovery:** - Drag-based canopy slows descent to terminal velocity $V_t$. - *Advantage:* Works over any terrain; emergency-capable. - *Application:* Target drones (BQM-74 Chukar), high-altitude balloons. 3. **Net Recovery:** - UAV flies into suspended fabric net; net absorbs kinetic energy. - *Advantage:* Allows shipboard operations with no runway. - *Application:* ScanEagle maritime ISR. 4. **VTOL / Vertical Recovery:** - UAV transitions to hover and descends vertically. - *Advantage:* Precision touchdown; no ground infrastructure. - *Application:* Urban delivery drones, multi-rotor UAVs. (Marks: 1.75 each for the four methods × 4 = 7) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Launch & Recovery Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting to compute canopy area from diameter** in parachute problems — many students plug in the diameter directly as $A$ instead of $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. This **doubles or halves** the answer and costs **2–3 marks** immediately. > > 2. **Mixing up stall speed with launch speed.** Launch speed must be *at or above* $V_{stall}$ — students often forget to verify this and lose the "feasibility" check marks. > > 3. **Ignoring g-load limits.** When asked to verify a launcher design, always comment on whether the launch acceleration is structurally acceptable (typically **$n \leq 10 \ g$** for UAVs without occupants). > > 4. **Assuming 100% energy transfer in bungee problems unless stated.** If the question says *"with 10% losses"*, the formula becomes $V_0 = x \sqrt{k/m} \cdot \sqrt{\eta}$ where $\eta$ is efficiency. Always read carefully. > > 5. **Omitting the free-body diagram** in Part B derivations — this **costs 1–2 marks** even if the math is right. > > 6. **Not specifying assumptions** (steady state, level flight, standard atmosphere) in derivations — examiners explicitly allot marks for assumption statements. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-revision checklist — re-read this the night before the exam:** - **Launch** = imparting sufficient kinetic energy to exceed $V_{stall}$ in a controlled, repeatable manner. - **Recovery** = controlled dissipation of kinetic + potential energy with no human on-board risk. - **Six launch methods:** hand, bungee, pneumatic/hydraulic catapult, runway, VTOL, air-drop. - **Six recovery methods:** belly, net, parachute, runway, deep-stall, VTOL. - **Key formula 1:** Bungee launch velocity $V_0 = x \sqrt{k/m}$ — assumes no losses. - **Key formula 2:** Parachute terminal velocity $V_t = \sqrt{2mg / (\rho C_D A)}$. - **Key formula 3:** Sink rate in glide $\dot{h} = V \cdot (D/L)$. - **Key formula 4:** Launch g-load $n = V_0^2 / (2 d g)$. - **Standard air density** $\rho = 1.225 \ kg/m^3$; **$g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$**. - **Acceptable landing sink rate** is **1.0–2.5 m/s** depending on airframe robustness. - **Approach speed** = $1.3 \times V_{stall}$ (industry rule). - **Stall speed formula:** $V_{stall} = \sqrt{2W / (\rho S C_{L,max})}$. - **Energy method** is the universal analysis tool — elastic PE → KE → aerodynamic work. - **Free-body diagrams + assumption statements** are non-negotiable in Part B derivations. - **Trade-offs to remember:** Impulse launchers minimize on-board energy but impose structural g-loads; VTOL eliminates launch/recovery infrastructure but reduces cruise efficiency. - **Always verify:** Launch velocity $\geq$ stall velocity; sink rate $\leq$ structural limit; g-load within airframe tolerance. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Launch and Recovery | PERUT631 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Hardware Anatomy of Quadcopter: Power Train and Propellers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT631 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hardware Anatomy of Quadcopter: Power Train and Propellers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Quadcopter Power Train?

The **Power Train** of a quadcopter refers to the integrated electromechanical system responsible for converting stored electrical energy from the battery into the mechanical rotational energy required to spin the propellers, which in turn generates the aerodynamic thrust that lifts and maneuvers the aircraft. It is the muscular heart of any multirotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV).

The standard quadcopter power train consists of four primary subsystems:
1. **Battery (LiPo)** — The energy reservoir.
2. **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)** — The power modulator.
3. **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** — The electromechanical transducer.
4. **Propeller** — The aerodynamic thrust generator.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT631 Module 2):**
> The power train is the series arrangement of *energy source → power distribution → motor controller → motor → propeller*. The efficiency of each stage multiplies, making the chain's weakest link the ultimate bottleneck for flight time and payload capacity.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Human Cardiovascular & Muscular System

Imagine a cyclist pedaling a bicycle up a hill:
- The **bicycle frame + fuel in the body** = the **LiPo Battery** (stores energy in chemical form).
- The **brain signaling the legs** = the **ESC** (decides how much energy to release, modulates the "voltage gate").
- The **leg muscles contracting** = the **Brushless Motor** (converts electrical pulses into rotational torque).
- The **bike wheel pushing against the road** = the **Propeller** (converts rotation into useful thrust).

If any one of these components is weak (e.g., a tired cyclist), the entire system fails. In a quadcopter, if you put a powerful motor with a weak ESC, the ESC will burn out. Similarly, a powerful motor paired with a small propeller will spin extremely fast but produce almost no thrust — wasting energy as heat and noise.

### 1.2 The Propeller — Aerodynamic Foil of the Quadcopter

A **propeller** is a twisted airfoil that converts the rotational mechanical power delivered by the motor shaft into a pressure differential in the surrounding air, thereby producing a reaction force (**thrust**) along the axis of rotation.

Key geometric parameters of a propeller:
- **Diameter (D)** — The tip-to-tip distance, usually in inches (e.g., 5", 10").
- **Pitch (P)** — The theoretical axial distance a propeller would advance in one revolution if it were screwing through a solid medium (in inches).
- **Number of Blades (B)** — Typically 2 or 3 for multirotors.
- **Blade Chord (c)** — The width of the airfoil cross-section at a given radius.
- **Twist Distribution** — The pitch angle varies along the span, optimized for uniform thrust distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Terminology:**
> A propeller is denoted as **"Diameter × Pitch × Blades"**, e.g., **1045 (10-inch diameter, 4.5-inch pitch, 2 blades)** or **5x4.3x3** for a 3-blade variant.

### 1.3 Direction of Rotation — CW vs CCW

In a quadcopter, two motors spin **Clockwise (CW)** and two spin **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)**. The propellers must match this rotation direction:
- **CW propellers** (often marked "R" or with an R-style leading edge).
- **CCW propellers** (often unmarked or marked "L").

This counter-rotation produces **zero net yaw torque**, allowing stable hovering. Reversing the propeller on a motor (or running a CCW prop on a CW-rotating motor) produces a fundamentally different aerodynamic effect, which is why matching is critical.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants for Power Train Analysis

The following constants and metrics are **universally used** in KTU-evaluated power train problems:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Air density (sea level) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Motor KV rating | $KV$ | **RPM per Volt** |
| Battery Cell Nominal Voltage | $V_{cell}$ | **3.7 V (LiPo)** |
| Battery C-Rating | $C$ | Unitless multiplier |
| Propeller thrust coefficient | $C_T$ | Dimensionless |
| Propeller power coefficient | $C_P$ | Dimensionless |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LiPo Battery — Energy Reservoir

### Operating Principle

A **Lithium Polymer (LiPo) battery** stores electrical energy through the reversible intercalation of lithium ions between a graphite anode and a lithium cobalt oxide (or similar) cathode. For multirotors, LiPo batteries are favored due to their exceptionally high **energy density (Wh/kg)** and **discharge rates (C-ratings)**.

### Key Battery Parameters

1. **Voltage (V)** — Determined by the number of cells in series (**S-rating**):
   - 1S = 3.7 V (nominal)
   - 3S = 11.1 V
   - 4S = 14.8 V
   - 6S = 22.2 V

   Note: A fully charged LiPo cell is **4.2 V**, while the safe cutoff is **3.0 V**. The nominal **3.7 V** is an averaged value.

2. **Capacity (mAh or Ah)** — Total charge stored. A **3000 mAh** battery can deliver 3 Amps for 1 hour, or 30 Amps for 6 minutes.

3. **C-Rating (Discharge Rate)** — The maximum safe continuous discharge current:
   $$I_{max} = C \times C_{capacity}$$
   A **2200 mAh 25C** battery can safely deliver:
   $$I_{max} = 25 \times 2.2 = 55 \text{ A}$$

4. **Energy (Wh)** — The total available energy:
   $$E = V_{nominal} \times C_{capacity}$$
   A 4S 2200 mAh battery holds:
   $$E = 14.8 \times 2.2 = 32.56 \text{ Wh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LiPo and not Li-ion?**
> LiPo has a higher **C-rating** (allowing burst currents of 25C–100C), which is essential for the high instantaneous current demands of multirotor motors during aggressive maneuvers. Li-ion has higher energy density but lower discharge capability.

## 2.2 The Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)

### Operating Principle

The **ESC** is a three-phase brushless DC motor controller that uses **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** signals, typically at **400 Hz** or higher, to sequentially energize the three phases of the BLDC motor. Modern ESCs use **FET (Field-Effect Transistor)** bridges to switch battery voltage to the motor phases.

### Key Functions
- **Decodes PWM/Oneshot/DShot input** from the flight controller.
- **Commutates the motor** — determines which two phases to energize based on rotor position.
- **Limits current** — protects the motor and battery from overcurrent.
- **Provides BEC (Battery Elimination Circuit)** — steps down voltage to 5V/3.3V for the flight controller.

### BLDC Commutation Sequence

A BLDC motor has **6 commutation steps** per electrical revolution. The rotor position is detected either via:
- **Sensorless (Back-EMF detection)** — Most common in multirotors.
- **Sensored (Hall Effect sensors)** — Used for smoother low-speed starts.

## 2.3 The Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor

### Operating Principle

The BLDC motor uses **permanent magnets** on the rotor and **electromagnetic coils** on the stator. By sequentially energizing the stator coils, a rotating magnetic field is created, which drags the permanent magnet rotor along with it — producing torque.

### Key Motor Equations

**Motor Speed (RPM)** is determined by the input voltage and the motor's **KV rating**:
$$RPM = KV \times V_{applied}$$

For example, a **920 KV motor on a 4S (14.8V) battery** spins at:
$$RPM = 920 \times 14.8 = 13{,}616 \text{ RPM (no-load)}$$

Under load, the actual RPM drops to approximately **80% of no-load RPM**.

**Motor Torque** is inversely related to KV:
$$\tau \propto \frac{1}{KV}$$
Thus, **low KV motors = high torque** (good for large propellers), and **high KV motors = low torque** (good for small propellers).

## 2.4 The Propeller — Aerodynamic Analysis

### Momentum Theory of the Propeller

According to actuator disk theory, the thrust produced by a propeller accelerating a mass flow of air is:
$$T = \dot{m} \times \Delta V$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate through the propeller disk and $\Delta V$ is the induced velocity change.

Expanding this using air density $\rho$ and disk area $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$:
$$T = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_0 (V_1 + V_2)$$

For a hover condition where far-field velocity is zero:
$$T_{hover} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_i^2 \cdot 2 = \rho A V_i^2$$

Solving for the induced velocity in hover:
$$V_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}$$

### Blade Element Theory

Blade Element Theory (BET) decomposes the propeller into infinitesimal radial strips, computing the lift and drag on each strip, then integrating over the blade span. For each radial element at radius $r$:

$$dT = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{rel}^2 c(r) [C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi] dr$$

$$dQ = r \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{rel}^2 c(r) [C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi] dr$$

Where:
- $V_{rel}$ = relative velocity of blade section
- $c(r)$ = chord at radius $r$
- $\phi$ = local inflow angle
- $C_L, C_D$ = lift and drag coefficients

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You will rarely be required to integrate the full BET equations. Most problems use the **Thrust Coefficient ($C_T$)** and **Power Coefficient ($C_P$)** approach, which is essentially a black-box empirical fit to BET results.

## 2.5 Propeller Performance Coefficients

The performance of a propeller is characterized by two dimensionless numbers that depend only on the propeller geometry and the advance ratio $J$:

**Advance Ratio:**
$$J = \frac{V_\infty}{nD}$$

where $V_\infty$ is the freestream velocity, $n$ is the rotational speed in revolutions per second, and $D$ is the diameter.

**Thrust Coefficient:**
$$C_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$$

**Power Coefficient:**
$$C_P = \frac{P}{\rho n^5 D^5}$$

**Figure of Merit (FM):** The ratio of ideal power to actual power consumed:
$$FM = \frac{P_{ideal}}{P_{actual}} = \frac{C_T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2} \, C_P}$$

A perfect propeller has FM = 1. Real propellers have FM between 0.5 and 0.85.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \rho A V_i^2$ | Thrust in hover via momentum theory | N |
| 2 | $V_i = \sqrt{T/(2\rho A)}$ | Induced velocity in hover | m/s |
| 3 | $P_{ideal} = T \cdot V_i$ | Ideal power in hover | W |
| 4 | $P_{ideal} = T^{3/2}/\sqrt{2\rho A}$ | Compact ideal hover power | W |
| 5 | $RPM = KV \times V_{applied}$ | Motor no-load speed | RPM |
| 6 | $I_{max} = C \times C_{cap}$ | Maximum battery discharge current | A |
| 7 | $E = V_{nom} \times C_{cap}$ | Battery energy | Wh |
| 8 | $C_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$ | Thrust coefficient | — |
| 9 | $C_P = P/(\rho n^5 D^5)$ | Power coefficient | — |
| 10 | $FM = C_T^{3/2}/(\sqrt{2} C_P)$ | Propeller Figure of Merit | — |
| 11 | $J = V_\infty/(nD)$ | Advance ratio | — |
| 12 | $\eta_{total} = \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{esc} \cdot \eta_{prop}$ | Overall powertrain efficiency | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Notation Alert:**
> In exam problems, $n$ may be expressed in **rev/sec** or **rev/min**. Always convert to **rev/sec** before substituting into $C_T$ or $C_P$ formulas, since these are derived in SI units. $D$ must be in **meters**, not inches.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ideal Hover Power from Momentum Theory

The **Hover Power Equation** is the single most important formula in multirotor performance analysis. Let's derive it completely from first principles.

### Starting Point: Actuator Disk Model

Consider an idealized propeller as an infinitely thin disk of area $A$ that imparts a uniform axial velocity increment to the air passing through it. Let:
- $V_1$ = velocity of air upstream of disk
- $V_2$ = velocity of air downstream of disk
- $V_i$ = induced velocity at the disk plane

By mass conservation through the disk:
$$\dot{m} = \rho A V_i = \rho A \cdot \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

The thrust force equals the rate of change of momentum:
$$T = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)$$

Substituting:
$$T = \rho A V_i (V_2 - V_1)$$

### Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation

Applying Bernoulli's equation across the actuator disk (assuming incompressible, inviscid flow, and neglecting the pressure difference across the disk itself):

Across the upstream streamline:
$$P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_+ + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2$$

Across the downstream streamline:
$$P_- + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_i^2 = P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

Subtracting these:
$$P_+ - P_- = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$

The thrust is the pressure difference times area:
$$T = A(P_+ - P_-)$$

$$T = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$

### Step 3: Solve for Hover Condition

In hover, $V_1 = 0$ and $V_2 = 2V_i$. Substituting:

$$T = \frac{1}{2}\rho A [(2V_i)^2 - 0^2] = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (4V_i^2)$$

$$T = 2\rho A V_i^2$$

Solving for $V_i$:
$$V_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2\rho A}}$$

### Step 4: Compute Ideal Power

The ideal power is the thrust multiplied by the induced velocity:
$$P_{ideal} = T \cdot V_i = T \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{2\rho A}}$$

$$P_{ideal} = \sqrt{\frac{T^3}{2\rho A}} = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2\rho A}}$$

This is the **fundamental BEMT-based hover power equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> The exponent of $\frac{3}{2}$ on thrust is critical. It means that doubling the weight of your quadcopter increases the required hover power by a factor of $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$, NOT by 2. This is why doubling payload dramatically reduces flight time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Propeller Thrust and Power from $C_T$, $C_P$

A common KTU exam problem gives you thrust coefficient and rotational speed and asks for thrust in Newtons. Let's walk through this.

### Problem Setup
A quadcopter uses a **10×4.5 inch propeller** spinning at **8000 RPM** in sea-level air. The thrust coefficient at hover is $C_T = 0.10$. Find the thrust.

### Step 1: Unit Conversion

Convert all quantities to SI units:

$$D = 10 \text{ inches} = 10 \times 0.0254 = 0.254 \text{ m}$$

$$n = 8000 \text{ RPM} = \frac{8000}{60} = 133.33 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 2: Apply the Thrust Coefficient Formula

$$C_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$$

Rearranging for $T$:
$$T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$

### Step 3: Substitute Values

$$D^4 = (0.254)^4$$

Computing step by step:
$$D^2 = 0.254^2 = 0.064516$$

$$D^4 = 0.064516^2 = 0.004162 \text{ m}^4$$

$$n^2 = (133.33)^2 = 17{,}777 \text{ (rev/s)}^2$$

$$\rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 = 1.225 \times 17{,}777 \times 0.004162$$

Multiplication order:
$$1.225 \times 17{,}777 = 21{,}776.8$$

$$21{,}776.8 \times 0.004162 = 90.61$$

Finally:
$$T = 0.10 \times 90.61 = 9.061 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4: Compute Total Quadcopter Thrust

For a quadcopter with 4 motors:
$$T_{total} = 4 \times 9.061 = 36.24 \text{ N}$$

To hover a quadcopter, $T_{total} = W = mg$. So the maximum payload mass is:
$$m = \frac{T_{total}}{g} = \frac{36.24}{9.81} = 3.69 \text{ kg}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Flight Time Estimation

### Problem Setup
A quadcopter uses a **4S 5000 mAh 25C LiPo battery**. The total hover thrust is 36.24 N (from above). The total power consumption in hover is **320 W**. Find the approximate flight time.

### Step 1: Battery Energy Calculation

Nominal voltage for 4S:
$$V_{nom} = 4 \times 3.7 = 14.8 \text{ V}$$

Battery capacity:
$$C_{cap} = 5.0 \text{ Ah}$$

Total energy:
$$E = V_{nom} \times C_{cap} = 14.8 \times 5.0 = 74 \text{ Wh}$$

### Step 2: Account for Usable Capacity

LiPo batteries should not be discharged below 80% to preserve lifespan. Usable energy:
$$E_{usable} = 0.8 \times 74 = 59.2 \text{ Wh}$$

### Step 3: Compute Flight Time

Assuming average power draw $P_{avg} = 320$ W in hover:
$$t_{flight} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{59.2 \text{ Wh}}{320 \text{ W}} = 0.185 \text{ hours}$$

Converting to minutes:
$$t_{flight} = 0.185 \times 60 = 11.1 \text{ minutes}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Propeller Figure of Merit

The **Figure of Merit (FM)** quantifies how close a real propeller's performance comes to the ideal.

### Step 1: Compute Ideal Power from $C_T$

From the thrust coefficient definition, ideal induced power in hover:
$$P_{ideal} = C_T^{3/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho^2 D^2 A^2}{2\rho A}} = C_T^{3/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho A}{2}}$$

A more standard form:
$$P_{ideal} = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}$$

### Step 2: Compute Actual Power from $C_P$

From the power coefficient:
$$P_{actual} = C_P \cdot \rho \cdot n^5 \cdot D^5$$

### Step 3: Define Figure of Merit

$$\boxed{FM = \frac{P_{ideal}}{P_{actual}} = \frac{C_T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot C_P}}$$

### Example Calculation
For a propeller with $C_T = 0.10$ and $C_P = 0.045$:

$$FM = \frac{(0.10)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2} \times 0.045}$$

$$(0.10)^{3/2} = (0.10)^1 \times \sqrt{0.10} = 0.1 \times 0.3162 = 0.03162$$

$$\sqrt{2} \times 0.045 = 1.414 \times 0.045 = 0.0636$$

$$FM = \frac{0.03162}{0.0636} = 0.497$$

This propeller is approximately **49.7% efficient** — typical for a low-pitch multirotor propeller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:**
> A Figure of Merit greater than 0.7 indicates an exceptionally well-designed propeller. Most off-the-shelf multirotor propellers fall in the 0.5–0.65 range. Fixed-wing propellers can achieve 0.85+.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guidelines

| Application | Recommended S-Rating | Motor KV | Propeller (D × P) | Typical FM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Micro quad (≤ 100g) | 1S–2S | 3000–5000 | 3×2 or 5×3 | 0.40–0.50 |
| 5-inch FPV racing | 4S–6S | 1700–2400 | 5×4.3×3 | 0.50–0.60 |
| 10-inch cinematic | 4S–6S | 700–1000 | 10×4.5 | 0.55–0.65 |
| Heavy-lift (> 5 kg) | 6S–12S | 300–500 | 15×5.5 or 17×5.8 | 0.60–0.70 |


## 4.2 Signal and Power Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER_FLOW["HIGH CURRENT POWER PATH"]
        nodeA1["BATTERY +"] --> nodeA2["PDB"]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3["ESC IN"]
        nodeA3 --> nodeA4["MOTOR WINDINGS"]
        nodeA4 --> nodeA5["PROPELLER"]
    end
    subgraph SIGNAL_FLOW["LOW CURRENT SIGNAL PATH"]
        nodeB1["FLIGHT CONTROLLER"] --> nodeB2["DSHOT 600 PROTOCOL"]

    end
```

## 4.3 Propeller Thrust vs RPM Curve (Conceptual)

This diagram shows the **quadratic relationship** between propeller thrust and rotational speed, which is fundamental to multirotor control.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["RPM INCREASES"] --> nodeB["BLADE TIP SPEED RISES V tip"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MASS FLOW RATE UP"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["THRUST T INCREASES QUADRATICALLY"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["POWER P INCREASES CUBICALLY"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["EFFICIENCY MAX AT CRUISE RPM"]
```

## 4.4 CW vs CCW Propeller Configuration

The standard "X-configuration" quadcopter propeller layout:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph QUAD_X["QUADCOPTER X CONFIGURATION TOP VIEW"]
        nodeFront["FRONT MOTOR M1 CCW PROP"]
        nodeRight["RIGHT MOTOR M2 CW PROP"]
        nodeBack["REAR MOTOR M3 CCW PROP"]
        nodeLeft["LEFT MOTOR M4 CW PROP"]
        nodeFront --- nodeRight
        nodeRight --- nodeBack
        nodeBack --- nodeLeft
        nodeLeft --- nodeFront
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Diagonal Pairing?**
> In an X-configuration, diagonally opposite motors spin in the **same direction**. This pairs a CCW propeller with a CW propeller on adjacent corners, allowing yaw torques to be neutralized at hover while still allowing differential thrust for roll and pitch control.
