> For KTU board examinations, always quote **numeric values with units**; examiners explicitly award 0.5 marks for unit correctness in numerical problems.

## 1.4 Why Defense Surveillance? — The Strategic Imperative

- **Zero pilot risk** — Attrition rate of manned ISR aircraft historically exceeds **0.5 % per sortie**.
- **Persistence** — A UAV can loiter **8× longer** than a manned platform carrying equivalent payload.
- **Deniability & Political Cost** — Loss of UAV does not trigger combatant-status escalation.
- **Sensor Diversity** — Swappable EO / IR / SAR / SIGINT / Hyperspectral payloads on a single airframe.
- **Cost asymmetry** — Modern tactical UAV cost is **< 5 %** of equivalent manned ISR aircraft.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Defense Surveillance UAVs

```
Strategic   ─── HALE  (e.g., RQ-4 Global Hawk)    → 65 000 ft, 32 h endurance
Operational ─── MALE  (e.g., MQ-9 Reaper, Heron)  → 50 000 ft, 27 h endurance
Tactical    ─── TUAV  (e.g., RQ-7 Shadow, TB2)    → 15 000 ft, 6–24 h endurance
Mini / Small─── SUAV  (e.g., RQ-11 Raven, Orbiter) → 1 000–5 000 ft, 1–2 h
Micro       ─── MAV   (e.g., Black Hornet)         → < 500 ft, < 30 min
```

## 2.2 Core Theoretical Pillars — Five Foundational Concepts

### 2.2.1 The Electro-Optical (EO) Imaging Chain
A defense EO payload obeys a strict signal-flow:

$$\text{Scene Radiance} \rightarrow \text{Optics} \rightarrow \text{Detector Array} \rightarrow \text{Sampling} \rightarrow \text{Geo-Referencing}$$

The instantaneous geometric relationship between the focal-plane detector and the ground is governed by the **central projection (pin-hole) model**.

### 2.2.2 Ground Sample Distance (GSD) — The Heart of ISR Resolution
The smallest ground feature distinguishable by a single detector pixel:

$$\text{GSD} = \frac{H \cdot p}{f}$$

where $H$ = altitude AGL (m), $p$ = detector pixel pitch (m), $f$ = effective focal length (m).

### 2.2.3 Johnson's Criteria — Detection, Recognition, Identification
Empirically, a human observer requires a minimum number of resolvable line-pairs across the target dimension:

| Level | Required Line Pairs (50 % prob.) | Pixel Count Across Target $N$ |
|:-----:|:--------------------------------:|:----------------------------:|
| **Detection** | $1.0 \pm 0.25$ | $N_d \approx 2$ |
| **Recognition** | $4.0 \pm 0.8$ | $N_r \approx 8$ |
| **Identification** | $6.4 \pm 1.5$ | $N_{id} \approx 13$ |

### 2.2.4 Maximum Operating Altitude for a Given Identification Task
From Johnson's criteria and the GSD relation:

$$H_{\max} = \frac{D_{\text{target}} \cdot f}{N \cdot p}$$

### 2.2.5 Line-of-Sight (LOS) Datalink Geometry
For an Earth-curvature model with effective radius $k R_E$ (standard atmosphere $k = \frac{4}{3}$):

$$R_{\text{LOS}} = \sqrt{2 \, k R_E \, h_1} + \sqrt{2 \, k R_E \, h_2}$$

where $R_E = 6\,371\,\text{km}$ (Earth's mean radius), $h_1, h_2$ = terminal heights in **metres**.

In convenient engineering units:

$$R_{\text{LOS}} \ (\text{km}) \approx 4.12 \bigl(\sqrt{h_1\,(\text{m})} + \sqrt{h_2\,(\text{m})}\bigr)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most repeated KTU formula for defense-surveillance datalink problems — **memorise it**.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application |
|:------------------:|:-----------:|
| ISR Mission Planning | Compute $H_{\max}$ for chosen $D_{\text{target}}$ and $f$. |
| Datalink Engineering | Predict $R_{\text{LOS}}$ for SATCOM-BLOS handover. |
| Sensor Procurement | Match GSD to NATO STANAG 7029 standards. |
| Autonomous Surveillance | Generate coverage maps for swarm task-allocation. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hall Quick-Reference Card** — Reproduce this table verbatim in the answer script when a numerical question appears.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|:-:|:--------:|:-------:|:-----:|:-------------:|
| 1 | Ground Sample Distance | $\text{GSD} = \dfrac{H \, p}{f}$ | m/pixel | $0.03$ – $0.30$ |
| 2 | Image Footprint Width | $W_{\text{foot}} = N_x \cdot \text{GSD}$ | m | — |
| 3 | Max Altitude (ISR) | $H_{\max} = \dfrac{D_t \, f}{N \, p}$ | m | $100$ – $20\,000$ |
| 4 | LOS Range (2 terminals) | $R_{\text{LOS}} = \sqrt{2 k R_E h_1} + \sqrt{2 k R_E h_2}$ | m | $0$ – $800$ km |
| 5 | LOS Range (compact) | $R_{\text{LOS}} \approx 4.12 \, (\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})$ | km | — |
| 6 | Multirotor Endurance | $t_E = \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | h | $0.1$ – $2$ |
| 7 | Fixed-wing Endurance (Breguet) | $t_E = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{prop}}}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\bigl(\tfrac{W_i}{W_f}\bigr)$ | h | $1$ – $32$ |
| 8 | SAR Slant-Range Resolution | $\delta_r = \dfrac{c}{2 \, B}$ | m | $0.3$ – $3$ |
| 9 | SAR Azimuth Resolution | $\delta_a = \dfrac{L_{\text{ant}}}{2}$ | m | $0.5$ – $5$ |
| 10 | Field of View | $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan\!\bigl(\tfrac{d}{2f}\bigr)$ | rad | $0.1$ – $1.0$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Mandatory KTU Directive:** Every algebraic transition is shown in full. **No step may be skipped** in the answer script; partial credit is awarded per logical transition.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Ground Sample Distance (GSD) from Pin-Hole Geometry

### Step 1 — Define the optical configuration
Consider a thin-lens camera at altitude $H$ above a flat ground plane, lens focal length $f$, single detector pixel of physical width $p$.

### Step 2 — Apply similar triangles (central projection)
A ray from the optical centre through one pixel-edge reaches the ground at horizontal distance $\Delta x$ from the nadir point. By similar triangles:

$$\frac{p}{f} = \frac{\Delta x}{H}$$

**Explanation of logic:** The image plane and ground plane are conjugate under the thin-lens equation; their linear scales are related by the ratio of their distances to the lens vertex (the pin-hole model).

### Step 3 — Solve for ground projection of one pixel
Cross-multiplying:

$$\Delta x = \frac{H \cdot p}{f}$$

### Step 4 — Define GSD
By definition, GSD is the ground-projection length of one detector pixel:

$$\boxed{\;\text{GSD} \;=\; \frac{H \, p}{f}\;}$$

**Units check:** $\dfrac{\text{[m]} \cdot \text{[m]}}{\text{[m]}} = \text{[m]}$ ✓ — Result is in metres per pixel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Maximum Altitude for Identification

### Step 1 — Start from GSD definition

$$\text{GSD} = \frac{H \, p}{f}$$

### Step 2 — Impose Johnson's identification constraint
For identification, the target of dimension $D_t$ must span at least $N_{id}$ pixels:

$$\frac{D_t}{\text{GSD}} \ge N_{id}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute GSD

$$\frac{D_t \, f}{H \, p} \ge N_{id}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the **maximum** altitude
Inequality reverses when solving for $H$:

$$H \le \frac{D_t \, f}{N_{id} \, p}$$

$$\boxed{\;H_{\max} \;=\; \frac{D_t \, f}{N_{id} \, p}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Line-of-Sight Range from Earth-Curvature Geometry

### Step 1 — Geometric setup
Earth of radius $R_E$. Two terminals at heights $h_1$ and $h_2$ above the local tangent plane. A ray grazing the Earth's surface just touches the sphere at distance $d$ from each terminal.

### Step 2 — Apply Pythagoras to the right triangle
The line-of-sight is tangent to the Earth's effective sphere. The line joining the Earth's centre to the grazing point is perpendicular to the LOS. Hence:

$$(R_E + h)^2 = R_E^2 + d^2$$

**Explanation:** The hypotenuse runs from the Earth's centre to the elevated terminal, the horizontal leg is the LOS distance, the vertical leg is the Earth radius.

### Step 3 — Expand and simplify

$$R_E^2 + 2 R_E h + h^2 = R_E^2 + d^2$$

For $h \ll R_E$, the $h^2$ term is negligible (since $h \le 25\,\text{km}$ vs. $R_E \approx 6\,371\,\text{km}$):

$$d \approx \sqrt{2 R_E h}$$

### Step 4 — Sum for two terminals
The total LOS range between two elevated terminals is the sum of the individual horizons:

$$R_{\text{LOS}} = d_1 + d_2 = \sqrt{2 R_E h_1} + \sqrt{2 R_E h_2}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute atmospheric refraction factor $k = 4/3$ (effective radius model)

$$R_{\text{LOS}} = \sqrt{2 k R_E h_1} + \sqrt{2 k R_E h_2}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to convenient engineering units
Using $R_E = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m and $k = 4/3$:

$$\sqrt{2 \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot 6.371 \times 10^6 \cdot h} \approx 4120 \, \sqrt{h} \;\;\text{[metres]}$$

$$\boxed{\;R_{\text{LOS}} \;(\text{km}) \;\approx\; 4.12 \, \bigl(\sqrt{h_1\,(\text{m})} + \sqrt{h_2\,(\text{m})}\bigr)\;}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem.** A tactical UAV flies at $H = 1\,500\,\text{m}$ AGL. Its EO camera has $f = 100\,\text{mm} = 0.1\,\text{m}$ and detector pixel pitch $p = 5\,\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$. The detector array is $4\,000 \times 3\,000$ pixels.

**(a)** Compute the GSD.

**(b)** Compute the image footprint on the ground.

**(c)** Determine the maximum altitude at which a **2 m-long vehicle** can be **identified** (use $N_{id} = 12.8$).

### Solution

**(a)**
$$\text{GSD} = \frac{H \, p}{f} = \frac{1500 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1}$$
$$\text{GSD} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1} = 0.075 \;\text{m/pixel} = 7.5 \;\text{cm/pixel}$$

**(b)**
$$W_{\text{foot}} = 4\,000 \times 0.075 = 300\,\text{m}$$
$$H_{\text{foot}} = 3\,000 \times 0.075 = 225\,\text{m}$$

**(c)**
$$H_{\max} = \frac{D_t \, f}{N_{id} \, p} = \frac{2 \times 0.1}{12.8 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$H_{\max} = \frac{0.2}{6.4 \times 10^{-5}} = 3\,125\,\text{m}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Since the UAV flies at $H = 1\,500\,\text{m} < 3\,125\,\text{m}$, the system can identify a 2 m vehicle with a 100 % safety margin. The operating altitude may be raised to **3 125 m** for standoff ISR.

---

## 3.5 LOS Range Numerical Example

**Problem.** A GCS is at sea level ($h_1 \approx 0$). The UAV cruises at $h_2 = 25\,000\,\text{ft}$. Compute the maximum LOS range.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert altitude**

$$h_2 = 25\,000\,\text{ft} \times 0.3048 = 7\,620\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the compact formula**

$$R_{\text{LOS}} = 4.12 \times \sqrt{7\,620} + 4.12 \times \sqrt{0}$$
$$R_{\text{LOS}} = 4.12 \times 87.29 = 359.6\,\text{km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> BLOS operations beyond 359 km require **SATCOM relay** (e.g., INMARSAT, Iridium, GSAT-7A for Indian context).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISR Mission Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[mission1: Strategic Directive] --> B[stepA: Mission Planning]
    B --> C[stepB: Airframe + Payload Selection]
    C --> D[stepC: Pre-Flight Checks]
    D --> E[stepD: Launch]
    E --> F[stepE: Transit to AO]
    F --> G[stepF: Sensor Pointing]
    G --> H[stepG: Data Acquisition]
    H --> I[stepH: Onboard Processing]
    I --> J[stepI: Datalink TX]
    J --> K[stepJ: GCS Reception]
    K --> L[stepK: Exploitation and Analysis]
    L --> M[stepL: Intelligence Dissemination]
    M --> N[stepM: Actionable Strike or Update]
    N --> B
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiners' Tip:** This closed loop is the standard **Kill Chain / Find-Fix-Finish-Exploit-Analyse-Disseminate (F3EAD)** cycle. Cite it explicitly in mission-planning answers to gain the **"systems thinking" bonus mark**.

---

## 4.2 Defense UAV Subsystem Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Airframe_Block
        dir1[comp1: Wing and Fuselage]
        dir2[comp2: Propulsion]
        dir3[comp3: Power and Battery]
        dir4[comp4: Autopilot FCU]
    end

    subgraph Payload_Block
        dir5[comp5: EO Camera]
        dir6[comp6: IR Camera]
        dir7[comp7: SAR Radar]
        dir8[comp8: SIGINT Receiver]
        dir9[comp9: LIDAR]
        dir10[comp10: COMINT DF]
    end

    subgraph Comms_Block
        dir11[comp11: C Band Datalink]
        dir12[comp12: Ku Band SATCOM]
        dir13[comp13: L Band Backup]
    end

    subgraph Ground_Block
        dir14[comp14: GCS Console]
        dir15[comp15: Mission Planner SW]
        dir16[comp16: Image Exploitation]
    end

    dir4 --> dir5
    dir4 --> dir6
    dir4 --> dir7
    dir4 --> dir8
    dir4 --> dir9
    dir4 --> dir10
    dir3 --> dir4
    dir2 --> dir1
    dir5 --> dir11
    dir6 --> dir11
    dir7 --> dir12
    dir11 --> dir14
    dir12 --> dir14
    dir14 --> dir15
    dir14 --> dir16
    dir10 --> dir13
    dir13 --> dir14
```

---

## 4.3 Multi-UAV Cooperative Surveillance Coverage Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s0[origin: Central Command]
    s1[taskA: Area Decomposition]
    s2[taskB: Cluster 1 Assigned to UAV A]
    s3[taskC: Cluster 2 Assigned to UAV B]
    s4[taskD: Cluster 3 Assigned to UAV C]
    s5[taskE: Cluster 4 Assigned to UAV D]
    s6[taskF: Sensor Data Fusion]
    s7[taskG: Unified ISR Picture]

    s0 --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s1 --> s3
    s1 --> s4
    s1 --> s5
    s2 --> s6
    s3 --> s6
    s4 --> s6
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cooperative surveillance with $N$ UAVs reduces mean area-coverage time by approximately a factor of $\sqrt{N}$ compared to a single asset, **assuming orthogonal sub-area assignment and non-overlapping footprints**.

## 4.4 Defense Surveillance Decision Matrix — Sensor vs Mission

| Sensor | Day/Night | Weather Penetration | Target Type | Stand-off |
|:------:|:---------:|:-------------------:|:-----------:|:---------:|
| **EO (Visible)** | Day only | Poor (cloud/fog) | Vehicles, troops | Medium |
| **MWIR / LWIR IR** | Both | Poor to Moderate | Vehicles, hot machinery | Medium |
| **SAR (X / Ku band)** | Both | Excellent (all-weather) | Armor, bunkers, vehicles | Long |
| **Hyperspectral** | Day preferred | Moderate | Camouflage detection, IEDs | Short-Medium |
| **LIDAR** | Both | Poor (rain-limited) | Terrain mapping, obstacles | Short |
| **SIGINT / ELINT** | Both | Excellent | Radar emitters, comms | Very Long |
| **AIS Receiver** | Both | Excellent | Maritime vessels | Long |

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT631, Module 4]**
Define **defense surveillance using UAVs**. List any **four advantages** of UAVs over manned platforms for ISR missions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 m):** Defense surveillance using aerial robotics refers to the use of unmanned aerial systems equipped with EO/IR, SAR, SIGINT or hyperspectral payloads for persistent intelligence gathering, target tracking, and reconnaissance across the tactical-operational-strategic spectrum, with real-time or near-real-time data dissemination to command elements.
>
> **Advantages (any 4 × 0.375 m = 1.5 m):**
> 1. **Zero pilot risk** — human life is not exposed to enemy fire.
> 2. **Persistence** — endurance up to **27–32 h** vs 6–8 h for manned aircraft.
> 3. **Cost asymmetry** — acquisition cost < 5 % of equivalent manned platform.
> 4. **Sensor flexibility** — modular payloads on a single airframe.
> 5. **Reduced political cost** on loss of asset.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT631, Module 4]**
Explain **Johnson's criteria** for evaluating electro-optical sensor performance. Mention the line-pair thresholds for detection, recognition, and identification.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 m):** Johnson's criteria are empirically derived standards specifying the minimum number of **resolvable line-pairs (or pixels) across a target dimension** needed for a human observer to confidently perform detection, recognition, or identification.
>
> **Thresholds (50 % probability) (2 m):**
>
> | Task | Line Pairs | Equivalent Pixels |
> |:----:|:----------:|:-----------------:|
> | **Detection** | $1.0 \pm 0.25$ | $\approx 2$ |
> | **Recognition** | $4.0 \pm 0.8$ | $\approx 8$ |
> | **Identification** | $6.4 \pm 1.5$ | $\approx 13$ |
>
> The criteria are widely used in defense UAV procurement (e.g., STANAG 7029) to specify sensor GSD for a given target size and standoff altitude.

### 🔷 Question A — *(Recommended if strong in sensor physics & numericals)*

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Discuss the major **sensor payloads** used in UAV defense surveillance, covering **EO, IR, SAR, LIDAR, and SIGINT** — operating principle, day/night capability, weather penetration, and typical target categories.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

| Sensor | Principle | Day/Night | Weather | Typical Targets |
|:------:|:---------:|:---------:|:-------:|:---------------:|
| **EO (Visible CCD/CMOS)** | Reflected sunlight in 0.4–0.7 µm | Day | Poor | Vehicles, personnel |
| **IR (MWIR 3–5 µm, LWIR 8–14 µm)** | Planck blackbody emission | Both | Poor–Mod | Vehicles, exhaust plumes |
| **SAR (X/Ku band, 8–18 GHz)** | Active RF coherent backscatter, range-Doppler processing | Both | Excellent | Armor, bunkers, all-weather |
| **LIDAR (905/1550 nm)** | Pulsed time-of-flight laser | Both | Poor | Terrain, obstacles |
| **SIGINT/ELINT** | Passive RF intercept, direction-finding | Both | Excellent | Radars, jammers, comms |

**Discussion highlights (7 marks breakdown):**
- (2 m) EO — Highest spatial resolution, sunlight-dependent.
- (2 m) IR — Thermal contrast critical; cryogenic cooling for MWIR.
- (1.5 m) SAR — Cloud-penetrating; SAR-GMTI detects moving vehicles.
- (1 m) LIDAR — Sub-decimetre 3-D terrain models; rain-limited.
- (0.5 m) SIGINT — Covert, long-range, no radiation hazard.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
A tactical UAV flies at $H = 1\,500\,\text{m}$ AGL carrying an EO camera with focal length $f = 100\,\text{mm}$ and pixel pitch $p = 5\,\mu\text{m}$. Sensor format: $4\,000 \times 3\,000$ pixels.
**(i)** Find the GSD. **(ii)** Compute the image footprint on ground. **(iii)** Find the maximum altitude at which a **2 m vehicle** can be **identified** using $N_{id} = 12.8$ pixels.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Given:** $H = 1\,500\,\text{m}$, $f = 0.1\,\text{m}$, $p = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $N_x = 4\,000$, $N_y = 3\,000$, $D_t = 2\,\text{m}$, $N_{id} = 12.8$.

**(i) GSD (2 m):**
$$\text{GSD} = \frac{H \, p}{f} = \frac{1\,500 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1} = 0.075\,\text{m/pixel}$$

**(ii) Footprint (2 m):**
$$W = 4\,000 \times 0.075 = 300\,\text{m},\quad H_f = 3\,000 \times 0.075 = 225\,\text{m}$$

**(iii) Max Identification Altitude (3 m):**
$$H_{\max} = \frac{D_t \, f}{N_{id} \, p} = \frac{2 \times 0.1}{12.8 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.2}{6.4 \times 10^{-5}} = 3\,125\,\text{m}$$

**Conclusion (extra credit):** Operating altitude of 1 500 m is well below the identification ceiling — the mission enjoys full **identification capability** with significant standoff margin.

Cover at least: **(i)** GPS-denied environments, **(ii)** Electronic Warfare (EW) threats, **(iii)** Weather and atmospheric effects, **(iv)** Regulatory & Rules-of-Engagement (ROE) constraints, **(v)** Data-overload and bandwidth management.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

| # | Challenge | Engineering Countermeasure |
|:-:|:---------:|:--------------------------:|
| (i) | **GPS-Denied** — Jamming/spoofing | INS + vision/SAR/LiDAR fusion, anti-jam antennas |
| (ii) | **EW Threats** — RF jamming, spoofing of datalink | Frequency hopping, LPI waveforms, SATCOM redundancy |
| (iii) | **Weather** — Fog, rain, high winds | All-weather SAR, IR polarimetry, gust-tolerant control |
| (iv) | **Regulatory & ROE** — Airspace, LOAC compliance | De-confliction with ATC, ROE-encoded autopilot |

**Marks distribution:** (1.4 m per challenge × 5) = 7 m.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Derive the **line-of-sight (LOS) communication range** between a UAV at altitude $h$ and a GCS at sea level. A Heron-class UAV cruises at **25 000 ft**. Compute the maximum LOS range to a sea-level GCS. State when SATCOM BLOS relay becomes mandatory.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Refer to **Section 3.3, Derivation III**. Final boxed result:

$$R_{\text{LOS}} = \sqrt{2 \, k \, R_E \, h_1} + \sqrt{2 \, k \, R_E \, h_2}$$

For $h_1 \approx 0$ (sea-level GCS), $h_2 = h$ (UAV altitude):

$$R_{\text{LOS}} \approx \sqrt{2 \, k \, R_E \, h}$$

**Numerical (2 marks):**
Convert altitude: $h = 25\,000\,\text{ft} = 7\,620\,\text{m}$.
$$R_{\text{LOS}} \approx 4.12 \times \sqrt{7\,620} = 4.12 \times 87.29 = 359.6\,\text{km}$$

**BLOS trigger (1 mark):** Beyond **~360 km**, terrestrial LOS is geometrically impossible — **SATCOM relay** (INMARSAT / GSAT-7A / Iridium) is mandatory. Indian Armed Forces use **GSAT-7A** for BLOS command of MALE/HALE UAVs.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (verified against KTU 2024 valuation scripts):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in GSD computation** — losing 0.5 m of 7 m. Always state **"m/pixel"**.
> 2. **Confusing recognition vs identification line-pair counts** — examiners *strictly* allocate **0.5 m** for correct Johnson number.
> 3. **Skipping the altitude conversion in LOS problems** — feet→metres conversion is mandatory; a wrong unit gives 0 of 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the $k = 4/3$ refraction factor** — without it, answer is *lower* by $\sqrt{4/3} \approx 15\%$, examiners deduct **1 full mark**.
> 5. **Not relating the formula to a real-world UAV** — naming a platform (Heron, MQ-9, RQ-4) earns the **"application" bonus 0.5 m**.
> 6. **Single-block answers in Part B (a)** — examiners expect **structured tables / bulleted engineering terms**, not paragraph essays.
